results.core_alignment_check: Processor obligation to inform the Controller of a legal requirement before processing, when processing is required by Union/Member State law and disclosure is not prohibited on important public-interest grounds, matches the regulation's same processor-to-controller notification duty.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are triggered when the processor is subject to a legal requirement to process, and both include the carve-out where law prohibits informing the controller on public-interest grounds.
results.modality_check: Equal or stronger: 'shall inform' is mandatory and matches the regulation's obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the timing 'before Processing' matches 'before processing'; the object 'that legal requirement' and recipient 'the Controller' align substantively.
results.contract_source_text: to Process Personal Data only in accordance with the instructions from the Controller, cf. clause 2 of this Agreement, including with regard to transfers of Personal Data to a Third Country or an international organisation, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law to which the Processor is subject; in such a case, the Processor shall inform the Controller of that legal requirement before Processing, unless that law prohibits such information on important grounds of public interest;

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: both clauses require the processor to inform the controller about intended changes involving addition or replacement of sub-processors under a general written authorization.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are triggered in the case of general written authorization and intended changes concerning addition/replacement of sub-processors.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract uses 'shall inform,' matching the regulatory obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the contract adds controller objection mechanics and a 30-day deemed acceptance rule, but these do not weaken the required notification duty.
results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: Processor informs Controller of intended changes concerning addition or replacement of sub-processors; same actor, action, object, and recipient as the regulation.
results.condition_check: Same triggering condition: in case of general written authorization.
results.modality_check: Mandatory 'shall' obligation, equal to the regulation's requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: Same subject matter and timing/content of notice; the added objection/acceptance mechanics do not weaken the notice duty.
results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: both require the processor to allow for and contribute to audits/inspections conducted by the controller or a controller-mandated auditor; the contract is the same duty with a narrower auditor description and added purpose/confidentiality limits.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are triggered in the audit/inspection context; the contract adds reasonable qualifiers (normal business hours, reasonably necessary, confidentiality) without weakening the duty.
results.modality_check: Compatible: 'to allow for and contribute' imposes an obligation at least as strong as the regulation's 'shall allow for and contribute'.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the contract's timing/location limits (during normal business hours) and auditor qualification/confidentiality conditions are additional constraints, not looser than the regulation.
results.contract_source_text: to allow for and contribute during normal business hours to reasonably necessary audits including inspections, conducted by an external qualified auditor mandated by the Controller, solely for the purpose of fulfilment of the Controller's obligations laid down in Article 28 of the GDPR and for accurately stipulated research questions in this connection provided that such external qualified auditor is subject to and bound by confidentiality obligations as stipulated in clause 12 of this Agreement;

results.core_alignment_check: Same actor (processor) and same obligation: ensuring authorized persons processing personal data are bound by confidentiality or statutory confidentiality duty.
results.condition_check: No additional triggering condition in either text; compatible.
results.modality_check: Contract uses an affirmative mandatory obligation ('ensures') matching the regulatory 'shall'.
results.other_constraints_check: Same target group and same confidentiality requirement; no looser timing, scope, or location constraints.
results.contract_source_text: Processor ensures that persons authorised to process the Personal Data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: both impose on the processor liability to the controller for sub-processor obligations.
results.condition_check: No condition difference.
results.modality_check: Mandatory shall on both sides.
results.other_constraints_check: Equivalent scope and target; 'its sub-processor' matches 'sub-processor's obligations'.
results.contract_source_text: Processor shall remain liable to the Controller for the performance of its Sub- processor's obligations.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the contract sub-item addresses the same security measure as Art. 32(1)(c) — restoring availability and access to personal data in the event of a physical or technical incident.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are triggered by a physical or technical incident and require timely restoration of availability/access.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the parent clause is mandatory ('shall implement') and the sub-item is part of that mandatory security-measures list.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: 'in a timely manner' matches the regulation's timing requirement, and the object/target is the same personal data availability/access restoration duty.
results.contract_source_text: the ability to restore the availability and access to Personal Data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident;

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the regulation requires technical and organisational security measures, including regular testing/assessment/evaluation of their effectiveness; the contract parent clause and sub-item expressly require the Controller and Processor to implement appropriate security measures and include a process for testing, assessing and evaluating effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring security.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are framed as security measures for processing, taking account of risk and the processing environment.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract uses mandatory 'shall implement' language and a mandatory security appendix/sub-item, which is at least as strong as the regulation's requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the contract's testing/assessment/evaluation is regular/process-based and aimed at ensuring security, matching the regulation's 'regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness' requirement.
results.contract_source_text: a process for testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the Processing. 2. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by Processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to Personal Data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. 3. The Processor hosts the data, including Personal Data, of the Controller via Microsoft Cloud Azure. The online hosting Services are delivered from data centres solely situated within EU, in Ireland and the Netherlands. 4. Microsoft Azure platform provides the security measures as described on www.Processor.com. 5. Particular security measures to be taken out by the Processor under this Agreement are specified in detail on www.Processor.com (Processor's Security and Privacy Protection Policies). Appendix 3 Costs for assistance and audits This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. In the event that Controller requires assistance from the Processor or any of the Processor’s Sub-processors pursuant to this Agreement, such assistance is charged as follows:

results.core_alignment_check: Processor obligation to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures for security/protection of personal data and data subjects' rights aligns with Art. 32(1)'s processor duty to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures ensuring security appropriate to risk for natural persons' rights and freedoms.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are general security/protection measures tied to GDPR compliance and protection of rights; the contract does not narrow the triggering situation.
results.modality_check: Equal or stronger: 'agrees to implement' is mandatory, not merely permissive.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the contract's broader phrasing ('meet the requirements of the GDPR and other applicable law' and 'ensure the protection of the rights of the Data Subjects') is at least as strict as security appropriate to risk; no weaker timing, location, or manner constraint appears.
results.contract_source_text: The Processor agrees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that the Processing of the Personal Data will meet the requirements of the GDPR and other applicable national and EU data protection law and ensure the protection of the rights of the Data Subjects.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires the processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational security measures appropriate to risk; the contract clause requires the Controller and the Processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, with examples such as encryption and resilience. Same real-world security obligation, and the contract is at least as strong.
results.condition_check: Compatible. Both are conditioned on the risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, with the contract adding standard contextual factors (state of the art, costs, nature/scope/context/purposes) that do not weaken the duty.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses 'shall implement', which is mandatory and at least as strong as the regulation's 'shall implement'.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The contract's 'appropriate technical and organisational measures' and 'level of security appropriate to the risk' match the regulation; the listed examples are additional specifics and do not narrow the obligation.
results.contract_source_text: Data relating to criminal convictions and offences (cf. GDPR art. 10). *Sensitive data includes: data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious and/or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, processing of genetic data or biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health and/or data concerning a natural person's sex life or sexual orientation Please note that for the processing of sensitive data, explicit consent of the Data Subject is required. Appendix 2 Security of Processing This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;

results.core_alignment_check: Processor assists the controller with DPIA-related compliance; same real-world obligation, and the contract also includes prior consultation of supervisory authorities where required, which is within the same assistance duty.
results.condition_check: Both are triggered by the controller's DPIA compliance needs; the contract's 'where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing' is compatible and narrower/expected.
results.modality_check: 'shall assist' is matched by an obligation to assist; not merely permission.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract adds a reasonable qualifier ('where necessary' and 'taking into account the nature of the Processing') and includes prior consultation assistance where required; this is not weaker than the regulation.
results.contract_source_text: where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing, to assist the Controller in its compliance with an obligation to carry out a data protection impact assessment (“DPIA”) and prior consulting of supervisory authorities where required, cf. articles 35 and 36 of the GDPR;

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires processor notification to the controller upon becoming aware of a personal data breach; the contract instead requires notification only if a supervisory authority contacts the processor, which is a different trigger and does not cover breach awareness
results.condition_check: Different condition: 'after becoming aware of a personal data breach' vs 'in the event that a supervisory authority contacts Processor'
results.modality_check: Obligation language is present, but it is tied to a different event and is qualified by 'insofar as permitted by law'
results.other_constraints_check: Timing/target differ materially; 'without undue delay' after breach awareness is not addressed
results.contract_source_text: To notify Controller in the event that a supervisory authority contacts Processor, insofar as permitted by law. 6. Personal Data breach

results.core_alignment_check: processor/controller assistance obligation: both require the processor to assist the controller, but the contract assistance is specifically for responding to data subject rights requests, not for ensuring the security of processing
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are processor assistance duties, with the contract adding a necessity/nature-of-processing qualifier
results.modality_check: Compatible: 'shall assist' is matched by an obligation to assist
results.other_constraints_check: Not aligned on object/purpose: regulation is security of processing; contract is assistance for chapter III data subject rights requests
results.contract_source_text: where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing, to assist the Controller by appropriate technical and organisational measures, insofar as this is possible, for the fulfilment of the Controller's obligation by law to respond to requests for exercising the Data Subjects’ rights laid down in chapter III of the GDPR;

results.core_alignment_check: Both address processor liability for damages, but the regulation is a specific GDPR liability rule limited to damage caused by processing when the processor breaches GDPR processor obligations or acts outside/contrary to lawful controller instructions. The contract instead imposes general liability for damages under Danish law subject to contractual limitations, without tying liability to the GDPR-specific conditions.
results.condition_check: Not aligned: the regulation's trigger is damage caused by processing plus specific GDPR-breach / unlawful-instruction conditions; the contract uses a broad general-law liability standard with clause-based limitations.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory liability language, but it is not the same or stronger obligation because it is framed differently and does not capture the GDPR-specific liability basis.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation has a specific causal and conditional scope tied to processing and unlawful conduct; the contract lacks those substantive constraints and instead refers to general Danish law and contractual limitations.
results.contract_source_text: The Processor shall be liable for damages in accordance with the general rules of Danish law subject to the limitations set out in this clause 13.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to inform the controller immediately when an instruction infringes GDPR or other data protection provisions. The contract requires the processor to inform the controller before processing if it is legally required to process contrary to instructions, but only about the legal requirement, not specifically that an instruction infringes GDPR/data protection law.
results.condition_check: Different trigger: regulation is triggered by an infringing instruction; contract is triggered by a legal requirement overriding instructions.
results.modality_check: Both are mandatory duties, but the contract duty is narrower in scope.
results.other_constraints_check: Timing is not equivalent: 'before Processing' is not the same as 'immediately' upon detecting an infringing instruction.
results.contract_source_text: to Process Personal Data only in accordance with the instructions from the Controller, cf. clause 2 of this Agreement, including with regard to transfers of Personal Data to a Third Country or an international organisation, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law to which the Processor is subject; in such a case, the Processor shall inform the Controller of that legal requirement before Processing, unless that law prohibits such information on important grounds of public interest;

results.core_alignment_check: Same actor (processor), same action (notify), same recipient (controller), same object (personal data breach awareness notification).
results.condition_check: Compatible: both trigger after becoming aware of a personal data breach.
results.modality_check: Compatible: contract 'will notify' is mandatory and matches 'shall notify'.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: 'without undue delay' matches exactly.
results.contract_source_text: Processor will notify the Controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a Personal Data Breach.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. Both provisions impose the processor's duty to ensure that persons acting under its authority with access to personal data process those data only on the controller's instructions, with the same EU/national-law exception.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The contract's exception for processing required under applicable EU law or national legislation matches the regulation's instruction-only rule as commonly qualified by law.
results.modality_check: Compatible. 'Must ensure' is at least as strong as 'shall ensure'.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The target group and scope are substantively the same: persons acting under the processor's authority with access to personal data, processing those data only on instructions from the controller.
results.contract_source_text: The Processor must ensure that the persons performing work for the Processor and who have access to Personal Data, only process such Personal Data as instructed by the Controller, unless processing is required under applicable EU law or national legislation. 13. Liability

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to ensure that any natural person acting under its authority with access to personal data processes them only on the controller's instructions. The contract requires the Processor itself to process personal data only on documented instructions from the Controller, and makes the agreement constitute those instructions. This is the same underlying instruction-only processing obligation, though the contract does not expressly mention natural persons acting under the processor's authority.
results.condition_check: Both apply to processing of personal data by the processor/its agents under the controller's instructions; the contract's documented-instructions framing is compatible with the regulation's instruction requirement.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory 'shall' and is at least as strict as the regulation's obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation's target is persons acting under the processor's authority with access to personal data; the contract is broader/stricter because it restricts the processor's processing generally to documented controller instructions, which covers and exceeds the required scope.
results.contract_source_text: The Controller hereby authorises the Processor to Process the Personal Data on behalf of the Controller on the terms and conditions set out in this Agreement. The Processor shall Process the Personal Data only on documented instructions from the Controller. The Parties agree that this Agreement shall constitute the instructions as of the date of the Agreement. The Processor may – unless otherwise specifically is set out in the Agreement – apply all relevant means, including software, servers and IT systems.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires the processor to assist the controller with prior consultation of supervisory authorities before processing in high-risk cases. The contract requires the processor to assist the controller with prior consulting of supervisory authorities where required, and ties this to GDPR Articles 35 and 36, which covers the same assistance obligation.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The contract limits the duty to 'where necessary' and 'taking into account the nature of the Processing,' which is not weaker than the regulation's high-risk / mitigation-triggered condition and is substantively aligned with the same situation.
results.modality_check: Compatible. 'Shall assist' is mandatory and matches the regulatory obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The contract's reference to DPIA and prior consulting of supervisory authorities under Articles 35 and 36 is substantively the same assistance context; it does not add a contradictory limitation.
results.contract_source_text: where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing, to assist the Controller in its compliance with an obligation to carry out a data protection impact assessment (“DPIA”) and prior consulting of supervisory authorities where required, cf. articles 35 and 36 of the GDPR;

results.core_alignment_check: Both clauses impose a processor duty to assist the controller, but the regulation is specifically assistance with prior consultation of supervisory authorities before high-risk processing, while the contract is assistance with responding to data subjects' rights requests under Chapter III. Different underlying obligation and recipient/task.
results.condition_check: Regulation triggers where processing would result in high risk absent mitigation measures; contract triggers where necessary and taking into account the nature of the processing. These are not the same situation.
results.modality_check: Both are mandatory assistance duties, but modality is not the issue here.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract's scope is limited to Chapter III data subject rights requests and uses 'insofar as this is possible'; the regulation concerns prior consultation of supervisory authorities before processing. Different object and target.
results.contract_source_text: where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing, to assist the Controller by appropriate technical and organisational measures, insofar as this is possible, for the fulfilment of the Controller's obligation by law to respond to requests for exercising the Data Subjects’ rights laid down in chapter III of the GDPR;

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the regulation requires regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational security measures, and the contract expressly provides for a process for testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the Processing.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both concern ongoing/regular evaluation of security measures; the contract does not narrow the triggering situation.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract states a process for testing/assessing/evaluating, which is at least as strong as the regulatory requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the subject matter, security objective, and scope of measures align; references to hosting, risks, and external security policies do not negate the core obligation.
results.contract_source_text: a process for testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the Processing. 2. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by Processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to Personal Data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. 3. The Processor hosts the data, including Personal Data, of the Controller via Microsoft Cloud Azure. The online hosting Services are delivered from data centres solely situated within EU, in Ireland and the Netherlands. 4. Microsoft Azure platform provides the security measures as described on www.Processor.com. 5. Particular security measures to be taken out by the Processor under this Agreement are specified in detail on www.Processor.com (Processor's Security and Privacy Protection Policies). results.core_alignment_check: Partial overlap: both concern the content of a personal data breach notification, but the contract only covers the nature of the breach and does not include the other required items (DPO contact details, consequences, mitigation measures).
results.condition_check: Compatible: both apply to the breach notification content; the contract's 'where possible' qualifier is not contradictory to the requirement for including the nature of the breach.
results.modality_check: Compatible: 'shall describe' is mandatory and at least as strong as the regulation's 'shall include' for the covered item.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible for the covered item: the contract addresses the notification content itself, with no conflicting timing, manner, or recipient constraint.
results.contract_source_text: The aforementioned notification shall describe the nature of the Personal Data Breach including where possible:

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to notify the controller of a personal data breach; the contract instead requires the controller to notify the processor of possible unauthorized use/security breaches related to the main agreement. The actor, direction, and object are different, and it is not the processor's breach-notification duty.
results.condition_check: Not aligned: the regulation is triggered by becoming aware of a personal data breach; the contract is triggered by becoming aware of any possible unauthorized use of credentials or other security breaches related to the agreement.
results.modality_check: Stronger timing/modality on the contract side ('shall immediately') but for a different obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Different recipient and subject matter; 'in writing' and 'immediately' are stricter, but the clause concerns controller-to-processor notice of security issues, not processor-to-controller notice of a personal data breach.
results.contract_source_text: The Controller shall immediately notify the Processor in writing after becoming aware of any possible unauthorised use of log-in information, passwords, credentials or other security breaches related to the Main Agreement. 7. Records of Processing activities

results.core_alignment_check: Processor assists Controller in fulfilling the Controller's obligation to respond to data subject rights requests; same real-world assistance duty, including chapter III rights requests.
results.condition_check: Where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing
results.modality_check: Shall assist / obligation, with 'insofar as this is possible' as a compatible qualifier; not weaker than the regulation.
results.other_constraints_check: Appropriate technical and organisational measures; same assistance context and target obligation, no contradictory timing/location/manner constraints.
results.contract_source_text: where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing, to assist the Controller by appropriate technical and organisational measures, insofar as this is possible, for the fulfilment of the Controller's obligation by law to respond to requests for exercising the Data Subjects’ rights laid down in chapter III of the GDPR;

results.core_alignment_check: The contract is a processor assistance duty to help the controller with GDPR compliance, but it is limited to DPIA and prior consultation of supervisory authorities; it does not address assisting the controller in ensuring processing security.
results.condition_check: The contract condition 'where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing' is compatible with the regulation's general assistance duty, but the underlying situation is different.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory 'to assist', which is not weaker in force.
results.other_constraints_check: The object/target is narrower and different: DPIA and prior consultation under Articles 35 and 36, not security of processing under Article 32.
results.contract_source_text: where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing, to assist the Controller in its compliance with an obligation to carry out a data protection impact assessment (“DPIA”) and prior consulting of supervisory authorities where required, cf. articles 35 and 36 of the GDPR;

results.core_alignment_check: Processor assistance to delete and/or return the Controller’s data including Personal Data overlaps with the processor’s duty to return or delete personal data to the controller after services end, but this clause is framed as assistance on request and includes export/return during and upon expiry of the Agreement rather than an unconditional processor obligation.
results.condition_check: Regulation triggers after the end of the provision of services relating to processing; contract triggers if Controller requests assistance during and upon expiry of the Agreement. This is related but narrower and conditional on a request, so it does not fully match the processor’s standalone duty.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a conditional assistance obligation ('if Controller requests... such assistance shall be rendered'), which is weaker than the regulation’s direct mandatory processor obligation to return or delete.
results.other_constraints_check: Timing is not fully aligned: the regulation requires action after the end of the provision of services relating to processing, while the contract refers to during and upon expiry of the Agreement and to export/return, not expressly all personal data deletion/return after service end.
results.contract_source_text: If Controller requests the Processor’s assistance to delete and/or return (i.e. export) the Controller’s data including the Personal Data during and upon the expiry of the Agreement such assistance shall be rendered by Processor at Controller’s expense and is charged under the conditions set out in Appendix 4. 10. results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: 'approved by the Controller' is substantively equivalent to prior written authorization; no weaker timing or scope shown.

results.modality_check: Mandatory assistance in both, with contract using 'shall assist' style obligation

results.contract_source_text: where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing, to assist the Controller by appropriate technical and organisational measures, insofar as this is possible, for the fulfilment of the Controller's obligation by law to respond to requests for exercising the Data Subjects’ rights laid down in chapter III of the GDPR;

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires the processor to return or delete all personal data to the controller after services end. The contract requires the processor, upon expiry, to delete all of the controller’s data including personal data, and also gives the controller access to export/return data during the agreement. The deletion obligation directly matches the regulatory duty; the return/export access is additional and not weaker.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The regulation triggers after the end of the provision of services relating to processing; the contract triggers upon expiry of the agreement, which is substantially equivalent. The storage carve-out for EU or Member State law is a reasonable legal exception and does not weaken the core duty.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses 'will delete', which is mandatory and at least as strong as 'shall return or delete'.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The scope covers all of Controller’s data including Personal Data, which is at least as broad as all personal data. The timing 'upon expiry' matches the end-of-services timing, and the legal-retention exception is consistent with GDPR-style retention limits.
results.contract_source_text: Processor will give Controller access to system functionality in order for the Controller to delete and/or return (i.e. export) any and all of Controller’s data including the Personal Data during the Agreement. Upon expiry of the Agreement the Processor will delete all of Controller’s data including the Personal Data, unless EU or national Member State law requires storage of the Personal Data.

results.core_alignment_check: Different assistance obligation: the regulation requires assisting the controller in responding to data subject rights requests, while the contract requires assisting with DPIAs and prior consultation of supervisory authorities.
results.condition_check: Both are processor assistance duties, but they are triggered by different underlying situations/tasks.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a mandatory assistance obligation ('to assist') with a qualifier ('where necessary and taking into account the nature of the Processing'), which is not weaker for its own subject matter.
results.other_constraints_check: The subject matter differs (data subject rights requests vs DPIA/prior consultation); no equivalent timing/location issue bridges that gap.

Appendix 2 Security of Processing This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to return or delete all personal data to/from the controller after the end of the provision of processing services. The contract sub-item, read with the parent context, concerns deletion or export of data upon the Controller’s request during the agreement and as an assistance service, not the mandatory end-of-services return/delete obligation.
results.condition_check: The triggering condition is different: regulation is after the end of the provision of services; contract is upon the Controller’s request and subject to separate payment.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a request-based assistance framework and payment condition, not an unconditional processor obligation to return or delete at service end.
results.other_constraints_check: Timing and target differ materially: the regulation is post-termination and covers all personal data; the contract is request-driven, fee-based, and framed as deletion or export assistance rather than the end-of-services handover/deletion duty.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to allow for and contribute to audits/inspections by the controller or a controller-mandated auditor. The contract clause only allocates who bears the costs and expenses of audits or inspections conducted by the controller or its representatives; it does not itself grant audit access, allow audits, or require contribution to audits.
results.condition_check: The triggering situation is audits or inspections related to compliance with Article 28 GDPR, which overlaps with the regulation's audits/inspections by the controller or its auditor, but the clause addresses only cost allocation for those events.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory allocation language for costs, but it is not the same modality as the processor's obligation to allow and contribute to audits.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract is about payment of reasonable costs and expenses for audits/inspections, not the manner of audit cooperation, access, timing, or scope of the processor's participation.
results.contract_source_text: payment of reasonable costs and expenses incurred during the course of providing a task or otherwise as a necessary part of such task or other assistance. 2. All costs and expenses of audits or inspections required and conducted by the Controller or its representatives in respect of the Processor’s or the Processor’s Sub-processors’ compliance with article 28 of the GDPR shall be borne solely by Controller unless otherwise specifically follows from the Agreement. results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires one possible security measure: restoring availability and access to personal data in a timely manner after a physical or technical incident. The contract sub-item states exactly that ability, with the same object (Personal Data), same trigger (physical or technical incident), and same timing (timely manner).
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are conditioned on a physical or technical incident.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract uses an affirmative security-measure obligation within a mandatory parent clause, which is at least as strong as the regulation's requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the timing and target are substantively the same; no weaker constraint appears.
results.contract_source_text: the ability to restore the availability and access to Personal Data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident;

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation imposes liability on any controller involved in processing for damage caused by infringing processing under GDPR; the contract instead makes the Processor liable for damages under Danish law and clause 13 limitations. Different actor, different legal basis, and different scope of liability.
results.condition_check: Not aligned: the regulation is triggered by damage caused by processing that infringes the GDPR; the contract is a general damages liability rule subject to Danish law and contractual limitations, not tied to GDPR-infringing processing by a controller.
results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory liability language, but it is for a different obligation and is not equal-or-greater for the regulation's specific controller liability duty.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract adds a different governing law and express limitations in clause 13, which materially narrow and redirect the obligation rather than matching the GDPR liability rule.
results.contract_source_text: The Processor shall be liable for damages in accordance with the general rules of Danish law subject to the limitations set out in this clause 13.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the regulation requires the breach notification to include the consequences of the personal data breach, and the contract sub-item states 'the likely consequences of the Personal Data Breach' within a notification that shall describe the nature of the breach.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both apply to the personal data breach notification; the contract's 'where possible' qualifier is not weaker than the regulation's baseline inclusion requirement for consequences.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the parent clause is mandatory ('shall describe'), and the sub-item is part of that mandatory notification content.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: 'likely consequences' substantially matches 'the consequences of the breach' and is not a looser or contradictory timing/manner/location constraint.
results.contract_source_text: the likely consequences of the Personal Data Breach;

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. Both provisions impose the processor's duty to inform the controller of a legal requirement before processing when processing is compelled by applicable Union or Member State law, subject to a public-interest prohibition exception.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The contract uses the same trigger: where the processor is required to process by Union or Member State law, and it adds the same carve-out that notice is not required if that law prohibits it on important/public-interest grounds.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses 'shall inform', matching the regulation's mandatory obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The timing 'before Processing' matches 'before processing'; the object and recipient are the same.
results.contract_source_text: to Process Personal Data only in accordance with the instructions from the Controller, cf. clause 2 of this Agreement, including with regard to transfers of Personal Data to a Third Country or an international organisation, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law to which the Processor is subject; in such a case, the Processor shall inform the Controller of that legal requirement before Processing, unless that law prohibits such information on important grounds of public interest;

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. Both address the processor’s security obligation for personal data processing: the regulation requires the processor to consider risk when determining security level, and the contract requires appropriate technical and organisational measures to protect personal data and meet GDPR requirements.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The regulation’s trigger is assessing security level in light of risks to personal data; the contract’s obligation is general and covers processing security measures, which is at least as broad.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses a mandatory commitment ('agrees to implement'), which is not weaker than the regulation’s 'shall'.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The contract is broader/stricter by requiring appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure GDPR compliance and protection of data subjects’ rights; it does not contradict or weaken the risk-based security requirement.
results.contract_source_text: The Processor agrees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that the Processing of the Personal Data will meet the requirements of the GDPR and other applicable national and EU data protection law and ensure the protection of the rights of the Data Subjects.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the regulation requires the breach notification to include the nature of the personal data breach, and the contract parent clause says the notification shall describe the nature of the Personal Data Breach; the sub-item 'the (estimated) time of the Personal Data Breach' is an in-line detail of that description, not a different duty.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both concern the breach notification content, with no conflicting condition.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the parent clause uses mandatory 'shall', so the sub-item is part of a mandatory notification description.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the contract adds a specific detail (estimated time) within the required breach-description content; this is not looser than the regulation's minimum content.
results.contract_source_text: the (estimated) time of the Personal Data Breach;

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to take the risk of accidental/unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure, or access into account when assessing security. The contract clause is about implementing appropriate technical and organisational security measures based on risk, including encryption and confidentiality/integrity/availability/resilience. This is the same security-risk obligation in substance, and it is at least as strong.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are triggered by assessing security in light of risk to personal data / rights and freedoms; the contract adds standard Art. 32 factors and is not weaker.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract uses 'shall implement' and therefore imposes a mandatory obligation equal to or stronger than the regulation's 'shall take into account'.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the contract's security measures are tied to the state of the art, implementation costs, nature/scope/context/purposes of processing, and risk, which aligns with the regulation's security-risk assessment context; it is not looser.
results.contract_source_text: Data relating to criminal convictions and offences (cf. GDPR art. 10). *Sensitive data includes: data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious and/or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, processing of genetic data or biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health and/or data concerning a natural person's sex life or sexual orientation Please note that for the processing of sensitive data, explicit consent of the Data Subject is required. Appendix 2 Security of Processing This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;

results.core_alignment_check: actor/action/object: regulation requires processor assistance to controller in ensuring security of processing; contract only authorizes processing on documented instructions and permits use of means, with no assistance/security obligation
results.condition_check: No matching security-assistance condition is stated
results.modality_check: Contract is permissive/authorizing for means and mandatory only for processing on instructions, not an obligation to assist with security
results.other_constraints_check: No equivalent security, assistance, or protective measures duty is imposed
results.contract_source_text: The Controller hereby authorises the Processor to Process the Personal Data on behalf of the Controller on the terms and conditions set out in this Agreement. The Processor shall Process the Personal Data only on documented instructions from the Controller. The Parties agree that this Agreement shall constitute the instructions as of the date of the Agreement. The Processor may – unless otherwise specifically is set out in the Agreement – apply all relevant means, including software, servers and IT systems.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor, when assessing security, to take into account specific risks to personal data (accidental/unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure/access). The contract instead imposes a confidentiality obligation on authorised persons; that is a different security measure and does not address the required risk-assessment obligation.
results.condition_check: Compatible in the sense that both concern security of personal data, but the contract has no risk-assessment trigger or assessment condition.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a mandatory obligation, but it is for a different duty.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation is about considering specified risks in assessing security; the contract is about confidentiality commitments of persons authorised to process data. Different object and action, not merely narrower wording.
results.contract_source_text: Processor ensures that persons authorised to process the Personal Data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the regulation requires regular testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational security measures, and the contract expressly provides for a process for testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the Processing.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both concern ongoing/regular evaluation of security measures; the contract does not narrow the trigger.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract states a process/obligation rather than mere permission, matching the regulatory duty.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the subject matter, target, and purpose align; the additional references to risk assessment and hosting/security details do not weaken the required testing/evaluation duty.
results.contract_source_text: a process for testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the Processing. 2. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by Processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to Personal Data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. 3. The Processor hosts the data, including Personal Data, of the Controller via Microsoft Cloud Azure. The online hosting Services are delivered from data centres solely situated within EU, in Ireland and the Netherlands. 4. Microsoft Azure platform provides the security measures as described on www.Processor.com. 5. Particular security measures to be taken out by the Processor under this Agreement are specified in detail on www.Processor.com (Processor's Security and Privacy Protection Policies). results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to impose Article 28(3)-equivalent obligations on engaged sub-processors by contract or other legal act. The contract clause only permits the processor to engage sub-processors for consultancy services; it does not require passing down GDPR/DPA obligations to them.
results.condition_check: The contract is conditional on the parties having entered into a Consultancy Agreement as part of the Main Agreement, which is not the same as the regulation's condition of engaging sub-processors and imposing obligations on them.
results.modality_check: The contract uses 'may', which is permissive. The regulation imposes a mandatory 'shall' duty.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract concerns engaging sub-processors for consultancy services, but omits the required contractual flow-down of the Article 28(3) obligations and any equivalent legal-act requirement.
results.contract_source_text: If the Controller and the Processor has entered into a Consultancy Agreement as part of the Main Agreement, the Processor may engage Sub- processors for the provision of the consultancy Services to the Controller.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: both concern processor-side assistance/implementation of security of processing measures to ensure appropriate security.

results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the contract's security measures (state of the art, risk-based, technical and organisational measures) align with the regulation's security-of-processing requirement and are not weaker.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to make available information to the controller to demonstrate compliance with Article 28 obligations. results.modality_check: The contract uses a mandatory obligation, but it is not the same obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Different target/recipient and different locus: supervisory authority cooperation versus information to controller for compliance demonstration.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: both concern the processor's responsibility for sub-processors' GDPR/data-protection obligations when engaging sub-processors.
results.condition_check: Compatible: the contract applies when the processor engages sub-processors for processing on behalf of the controller; the regulation is a general liability rule for sub-processor obligations.
results.modality_check: Weaker on liability: the regulation requires the processor to remain fully liable to the controller, while the contract requires flow-down of the same obligations to the sub-processor but does not expressly preserve the processor's full liability.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract adds a flow-down/contractual-imposition requirement and sufficient guarantees for technical and organisational measures; these do not substitute for the explicit liability obligation.
results.contract_source_text: In the event that Processor engages Sub-processors for carrying out Processing activities on behalf of the Controller in accordance with Appendix 5, the same data protection obligations as set out in this Agreement shall be imposed on that Sub-processor by way of a contract or other legal act under EU or national Member State law, in particular providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of this Agreement.

results.core_alignment_check: controller right to terminate the DPA vs clause giving each party entitlement to terminate the agreement in specified GDPR/data-protection-law change circumstances; same real-world termination right, though broader in who may exercise it
results.condition_check: Compatible: termination is triggered if the parties cannot agree on reasonable terms for implementing changes necessary for GDPR or other applicable data protection laws
results.modality_check: Equal-or-greater force: 'shall be entitled to terminate' grants a right, which meets a requirement that the controller have the right to terminate
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: written notice of 60 days and automatic termination of related personal-data agreements are additional constraints, not weaker than the regulation
results.contract_source_text: The Processor shall use reasonable endeavours to comply with any legislative changes. However, the Processor shall not be obligated to implement any change of the instructions if the Parties cannot in good faith agree to reasonable terms for the implementation. If the Parties fail to agree in good faith to reasonable terms regarding change of the instructions each Party shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement with a written notice of 60 days, provided that such changes are deemed necessary to comply with the GDPR or other applicable EU or national data protection laws and regulation. The Main Agreement and any other agreement between the Parties involving Processing of Personal Data shall automatically terminate at the same time.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires that, under a general written authorization for sub-processors, the controller has a right to object to intended changes involving addition or replacement of sub-processors after being informed by the processor. results.modality_check: The regulation requires a controller right to object. The contract provides authorization to the processor to use sub-processors, which is not an objection right and is materially different.
results.other_constraints_check: The required timing/sequence is notice of intended changes followed by an objection opportunity. The contract only mentions a list of sub-processors available at the date of the agreement and no change-notice or objection mechanism.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. results.core_alignment_check: Yes. This is the same security-risk assessment/implementation obligation and is at least as protective.

results.modality_check: Compatible. The regulation uses 'shall take into account'; the contract uses a mandatory 'shall implement' obligation, which is stronger.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The regulation lists specific risks to personal data; the contract addresses security appropriate to risk and includes measures directly targeting confidentiality, integrity, availability, resilience, and encryption of transmitted personal data, which meets or exceeds the required security focus.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational security measures to ensure risk-appropriate security. The sub-item about restoring availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident is a specific security measure within the same obligation.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are triggered by security risk / physical or technical incidents affecting availability and resilience.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract uses mandatory 'shall implement' within the parent clause, which is at least as strong as the regulation's obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: 'restore the availability and access to Personal Data in a timely manner' is substantively aligned with ensuring availability/resilience; timely manner is not weaker than the risk-based security requirement.

This is the same substantive security obligation and is at least as broad.

results.contract_source_text: Data relating to criminal convictions and offences (cf. GDPR art. 10). *Sensitive data includes: data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious and/or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, processing of genetic data or biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health and/or data concerning a natural person's sex life or sexual orientation Please note that for the processing of sensitive data, explicit consent of the Data Subject is required. Appendix 2 Security of Processing This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires prior specific or general written authorization from the controller before engaging a sub-processor, while the contract merely states that Microsoft is a sub-processor used for hosting services and where data are hosted; it does not itself grant or require prior written authorization.

results.contract_source_text: The Processor has entered into an agreement with Microsoft Corporation whereby Microsoft is a Sub-processor to the Processor acting (on the Controller's behalf) to provide hosting services via the Microsoft Azure Cloud Platform. results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the timing/term is expressed as lasting for as long as processing continues, which is at least as specific as the required duration.

results.contract_source_text: Data relating to criminal convictions and offences (cf. GDPR art. 10). *Sensitive data includes: data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious and/or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, processing of genetic data or biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health and/or data concerning a natural person's sex life or sexual orientation Please note that for the processing of sensitive data, explicit consent of the Data Subject is required. Appendix 2 Security of Processing This Appendix forms part of the Agreement. 1. Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of Processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Controller and the Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) encryption of Personal Data when transmitted via public networks and in connection with remote access to Controller’s systems; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of Processing systems and Services;

results.core_alignment_check: Both provisions concern processor liability for damage/loss, but the regulation is a specific GDPR rule limiting when the processor is liable for damage caused by processing: only for breach of processor-directed GDPR obligations or acting outside/contrary to controller instructions. results.contract_source_text: In the case of general written authorisation, the Processor shall inform the Controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, thereby giving the Controller the opportunity to object to such changes. However, the Controller cannot object to any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors, if the new Sub-processor provides sufficient guarantees with respect to implementation of appropriate technical and organizational measures in such a manner that the Processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR as outlined in this Agreement. If the Controller does not object to changes concerning the addition or replacement of Sub-processors within 30 days from the Processor’s notification of the intended changes, such changes shall be deemed to be accepted by the Controller.

results.contract_source_text: The Processor agrees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that the Processing of the Personal Data will meet the requirements of the GDPR and other applicable national and EU data protection law and ensure the protection of the rights of the Data Subjects.
